title: Delivery Framework Flow
summary: A strong system design answer needs a clear sequence from scope to deep dives.
task: Turn an ambiguous product prompt into requirements, estimates, architecture, and focused technical depth.
constraints: Time is limited, so the answer needs a track to run on
Requirements should narrow the design before components are drawn
Depth should be spent on the riskiest parts, not evenly across everything

qa.source_kind: Hello Interview In a Hurry framework chapter

blocks.title: Clarify scope first
blocks.body: Start by pinning down users, core actions, and explicit non-goals. This prevents spending design time on features the system does not need.

blocks.title: Functional + non-functional requirements
blocks.body: Separate what the product must do from qualities like consistency, latency, scale, and availability. These qualities drive the architecture.

blocks.title: Data model reveals boundaries
blocks.body: Core entities and relationships expose where ownership, indexing, and consistency boundaries should live. The model should support the main flows.

blocks.title: High-level design connects flows
blocks.body: Draw the minimum services, storage, cache, queues, and APIs needed to satisfy the requirements. Keep the diagram readable before going deep.

blocks.title: Deep dive on the highest-risk piece
blocks.body: Spend depth where failure would break the product: consistency, hot keys, fanout, idempotency, recovery, or cost. Do not distribute depth evenly.

callouts.title: Why this matters
callouts.body: The framework is not ceremony; it protects time and makes tradeoffs explicit before implementation details take over.

talk_track: For a framework-style chapter, I would make the answer procedural but still concrete. I would first clarify scope and requirements, then use the data model to expose ownership and consistency boundaries. After that I would draw a readable high-level design and choose one or two risky areas for depth, such as idempotency, hot keys, fanout, or recovery. The important part is not following steps mechanically; it is using the steps to spend time on the decisions that change the architecture.
